coredata.dc:title: Electric-field control of the degenerate shell in a triangular graphene quantum dot
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Magnetism and Magnetic Materials
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Qingrui
Jiang, Fan
Lian, Sen
Liu, Chunxiang
coredata.dc:description: For a triangular graphene quantum dot with up to two electrons filled in the degenerate shell, we calculate the eigenstates as functions of an electric field, using the tight-binding method, the Hartree-Fock method and the exact-diagonalization method. When the electric field is weaker, the eigenstates of the degenerate shell mix with each other. As the electric field becomes stronger, the eigenstates of the degenerate shell tend to be stable. We focus on the evolution of both the ground levels and the total spins in the electric field. Based on analyzing the chemical potentials, we present the control scheme of the shell filling and the total spin by the electric field. The results support the possibility to use the system as a spin memory device or an electron-transport switch.

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Magnetism and Magnetic Materials

N

= 1
states in the electric field

N

= 2
states in the electric field

Electric-field control of the electron number
N
el

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
(a) The TGQD (
N
edge
= 8
) subjected to the electric field generated by two gates with
± U
. The contour of the electrostatic potential is shown by green dashed curves. The leads S and D are also labelled. (b) The evolution of the degenerate shell in the electric field. The HF spectra are shown by blue curves and the TB spectra by black curves. The break in degeneracy gives rise to 7 states. Actually, at zero field, the number of degenerate states in a zigzag graphene triangle is
N
edge
- 1
[1]. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The evolution of the low-lying
N
el
= 2
spectra in the electric field. The
S = 0
spectra are shown by blue curves and the
S = 1
spectra by red curves. The spectra are obtained by the exact diagonalization. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) The evolutions of
μ ( 1 )
and
μ ( 2 )
in the electric field. The horizontal dotted line indicates the value of the chemical potential of the leads. (b) Schematic diagrams of the chemical potentials of the dot and leads, illustrating the change of
N
el
. The electron transport is activated at
μ ( N ) ≈
μ
leads
.

head.ce:title: Electric-field control of the degenerate shell in a triangular graphene quantum dot

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Electronics, Shandong Normal University, Jinan, Shandong, 250014, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For a triangular graphene quantum dot with up to two electrons filled in the degenerate shell, we calculate the eigenstates as functions of an electric field, using the tight-binding method, the Hartree-Fock method and the exact-diagonalization method. When the electric field is weaker, the eigenstates of the degenerate shell mix with each other. As the electric field becomes stronger, the eigenstates of the degenerate shell tend to be stable. We focus on the evolution of both the ground levels and the total spins in the electric field. Based on analyzing the chemical potentials, we present the control scheme of the shell filling and the total spin by the electric field. The results support the possibility to use the system as a spin memory device or an electron-transport switch.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are very interesting systems not only for their unique physical properties, but also for great application potential in electronics and optoelectronics. The electronic and magnetic properties of GQDs depend strongly on the atomic termination of the edges [1]. The presence of edge states in zigzag GQDs, not in armchair GQDs, was predicted theoretically [2] and confirmed experimentally [3]. Moreover, for triangular GQDs (TGQDs), there appears a degenerate shell of electronic states at the Fermi energy well separated in energy from the valence and conduction bands [2]. This allows to design a strongly correlated electronic system as a function of the shell filling, which is similar to the fractional quantum Hall effect.

Modern nanofabrication methods allow to tailor graphene with atomic precision [4] and allow to create a GQD close to a triangular shape [5]. The development of nanofabrication techniques triggered a lot of theoretical studies on the degenerate shell, including electronic structures [6–8], transport properties [9,10], optical properties [11–13] and external field control [14–19]. The electronic properties of large GQDs can be studied by solving the Dirac equation with the infinite-mass boundary condition [20]. Only atomistic approaches can capture the combined effects of edges and symmetry-breaking for smaller GQDs. The electronic properties of TGQDs with a half-filled shell have been studied using the atomistic tight-binding (TB) method [21]. The basic TB model are based on the assumption that the electronic occupation on every site is equal to one, which may not be true at the edges of a quantum dot.

To take into account the edge electronic occupation, the half-filled degenerate shell of a TGQD has been investigated using the mean-field Hubbard model [6,22], density functional theory (DFT) [7] and Hartree-Fock (HF) methods [23]. These calculations show that the ground states of TGQDs are fully spin polarized with a finite magnetic moment proportional to the shell degeneracy. For a fractional filling of the degenerate shell, the average electronic occupation on each site can be higher or lower than one. Thus, one must take into account the deviation of the electronic density from the bulk values due to both the fractional filling of the degenerate shell and the edge effects [21]. Except for the half-filling of the degenerate shell, only a fully interacting model can correctly capture the correlations among electrons occupying the shell. The fractionally filled degenerate shell has been calculated with the correlation effects using the configuration-interaction method [8]. Based on the calculation of the correlation effects, it is possible to control the electron filling of the degenerate shell, which is a prerequisite for related device applications.

In this paper, for the TGQD with up to two electrons filled in the degenerate shell, we calculate the eigenstates and the chemical potentials as functions of an electric field. Our goal is to control the number of the electrons (
N
el
) occupying the degenerate shell by the electric field. The paper is organized as follows. In Section 2, we calculate the HF quasi-particle eigenstates. In Section 3, we obtain the
N
el
= 2
states by exact diagonalization. In Section 4, we analyze the evolution of the chemical potentials in the electric field and design the
N
el
control scheme. In Section 5, we summarize our results.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
The evolution of the
N
el
= 1
states in the electric field
ce:section.ce:para:
A uniform in-plane electric field is applied to the TGQD with the size
N
edge
= 8
, as shown in Fig. 1 (a). The electric field is generated by two gate electrodes with opposite electrostatic potentials
± U
. The electron filling of the TGQD can be tuned by the voltages of the source and drain leads. In the section, we calculate quasi-particle states with a fully occupied valence band and a completely empty degenerate shell. For small triangles, the valence band and the degenerate shell are separated by a large energy gap [11]. Thus the interacting of valence electrons is expected to be well described in a mean-field approximation. These quasi-particle states can be considered as the
N
el
= 1
states. Moreover, as we shall see in the next section, these quasi-particle states are used to construct the basis set for calculating the
N
el
= 2
states.

The edge effects lead to the deviation of the electronic density from the bulk values. Moreover, in order to obtain an empty degenerate shell, the average electronic occupation on each site becomes lower than one. The self-consistent HF approach is an appropriate approach to these electron density deviations. The corresponding Hamiltonian can be expressed as [8]
(1)
H =
∑
i σ
U
i
c
i σ
+
c
i σ
+
∑
il σ
t
il σ
c
i σ
+
c
l σ
+
∑
il σ
∑
jk
σ
′
(
ρ
jk
σ
′
-
ρ
jk
σ
′
o
) ( 〈 ij | V | kl 〉 - 〈 ij | V | lk 〉
δ
σ
σ
′
)
c
i σ
+
c
l σ
where
c
i σ
+
(
c
i σ
) creates (annihilates) an electron with spin
σ
at site i,
U
i
is the gate electrostatic potential,
t
il σ
is a hopping integral,
ρ
jk
σ
′
is the density matrix element for the GQD,
ρ
jk
σ
′
o
is for the charge-neutral bulk graphene, and
〈 ij | V | kl 〉
is the two-body Coulomb matrix element with more details in Ref. [1]. The Hamiltonian given by Eq. (1) can be solved self-consistently to obtain HF quasi-particle states with a fully occupied valence band and a completely empty degenerate shell. In each self-consistent step,
ρ
jk
σ
′
is calculated by including the whole valence band and excluding the degenerate shell.

Fig. 1(b) shows the evolution of the
N
el
= 1
HF energy spectra in the electric field. As a comparison, the energy spectra obtained by the TB method are also shown, which are for both a half-filled degenerate shell and uniform electronic occupations. As the electrons in the degenerate shell are removed by the leads, the total interaction energy decreases. Thus, on the whole, the HF spectra is lower than the TB spectra. In the HF spectra, there are some splittings at
U = 0
, which shows that the degeneracy partly disappears due to the edge effects. For
U < 0.1
eV the levels tend to be close to each other and there are anticrossings between these levels, which implies that the eigenstates mix with each other. As U increases further, the levels trend to be far away from each other and to be linear with U, which implies that the eigenstates do not change significantly anymore [24]. Within the range of U under investigation, the ground level decreases linearly with U. The HF quasi-particle eigenstates keep spin unpolarized, which is similar to the situation without electric fields. Within the range
U < 0.5
eV, the energy gap between the shell and the lower/higher band is more than 1.1 eV. The value of the energy gap implies that the shell is still well separated in energy from the valence and conduction bands.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
The evolution of the
N
el
= 2
states in the electric field
ce:section.ce:para:
After obtaining the HF quasi-particle eigenstates, we now investigate the evolution of the
N
el
= 2
states in the electric field. The exact diagonalization for the Hamiltonian matrix is the most accurate and direct way of solving the many-body Schrödinger equation. Although computationally very demanding, the method captures all correlation effects missing in HF and DFT calculations. We model the TGQD by a two-electron Hamiltonian
(2)
H =
H
1
+
H
2
+
V
int
,
where
H
1
(
H
2
)
is the single-electron Hamiltonian for the HF quasi-particles and remains in the formalism of Eq. (1),
V
int
=
e
2
/ ( 4 π κ |
r
2
-
r
1
| )
is the interaction between two quasi-particles,
κ
is the effective dielectric constant and we take
κ = 6
according to the substrate [25]. In the previous section, we have obtained the HF eigenstates
ϕ
i
of the single-electron part of Eq. (2). The
N
el
= 2
states with the total spin S can be expressed as [26]
(3)
Ψ
S
(
r
1
,
r
2
) =
∑
i ⩽ j
α
i , j
{
ϕ
i
(
r
1
)
ϕ
j
(
r
2
) +
( - 1 )
S
ϕ
i
(
r
2
)
ϕ
j
(
r
1
) } .
The coefficient vector
α
l
for the lth eigenstate and the corresponding energy
E
l
can be computed by the exact diagonalization of the Hamiltonian matrix. Since S is a good quantum number, the Hamiltonian matrix can be divided into two blocks with different S.

Within the range of U under investigation, there exists a large energy gap between the shell and the conduction/valence band. Thus, many-body properties of electrons occupying the degenerate shell are primarily governed by the interactions between these electrons. In our calculations, we neglect scattering related to the conduction/valence band. In other words, the two-electron configuration in Eq. (3) is consisting of only the HF eigenstates from the degenerate shell. The validity of the approximation can be assessed by checking the convergence between the approximate computation and the computation using more HF eigenstates in Eq. (3). The approximation can significantly reduce the dimension of the Hilbert space.

Fig. 2 shows that the evolution of the
N
el
= 2
spectra in the electric field. There are no anticrossings between the levels with
S = 0
and those with
S = 1
, which implies that the
S = 0
eigenstates and the
S = 1
eigenstates evolute independently in two subspaces. For
U < 0.2
eV there are many anticrossings between the levels of the same S, which implies that the corresponding eigenstates mix drastically with each other. As U increases further, the levels tend to be far away from each other and there are fewer and fewer anticrossings. As the level spacings increase, the levels tend to be linear with U, which implies that the eigenstates tend to be stable [24]. In stark contrast to the other levels, the ground level keeps decreasing smoothly and monotonously. The electric field tends to remove the strong correlation of the degenerate shell and thus to remove the spin polarization [27]. In our calculation, the energy difference of the two lowest quasi-particle states (see Fig. 1) is smaller than the exchange energy and thus the ground state remains
S = 1
, which implies that the strength of the electric field does not fundamentally destroy the correlation of two quasi-particles. The energy gap between the ground level and the first excited level changes between 8 and 15 meV, which is helpful to the stability of the ground state.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Electric-field control of the electron number
N
el
ce:section.ce:para:
The number of electrons filled in a quantum dot can be determined by the condition that the chemical potential of the dot is less than those of the leads [28]. The linear regime of conductance is considered, implying
μ
S
-
μ
D
= - | e | V ≈ 0
, where
μ
S
and
μ
D
are the chemical potentials of the source and drain leads. The related literature can be consulted for the effects of arbitrary source-drain bias on the transport properties of quantum dots [29]. Since
μ
S
is approximately equal to
μ
D
, for convenience, the approximate value of
μ
S
and
μ
D
is denoted by
μ
leads
. The chemical potential of a quantum dot
μ ( N )
is defined by
μ ( N ) =
E
G
( N ) -
E
G
( N - 1 )
, where
E
G
( N )
is the ground state energy of the N-electron system. In the previous section, we have calculated the dependence of
E
G
( 1 )
and
E
G
( 2 )
on U. In the
N
el
= 1
system,
E
G
( 1 )
decreases linearly with U (Fig. 1) and the ground state remains
S = 1 / 2
. In the
N
el
= 2
system,
E
G
( 2 )
also decreases with U (Fig. 2) and the ground state remains
S = 1
. Fig. 3 (a) shows the evolutions of
μ ( 1 )
and
μ ( 2 )
in the electric field. Due to
E
G
( 0 ) ≡ 0
, the dependence of
μ ( 1 )
on U is the same as that of
E
G
( 1 )
.
μ ( 2 )
decreases more slowly with U than
μ ( 1 )
.

Now we display the control procedure of
N
el
based on analyzing the variation of the chemical potentials with U. To display the process of deducing
N
el
, the schematic diagrams of the chemical potentials are shown in Fig. 3(b). When
μ
leads
keeps constant in the range about from
- 0.42
eV to
- 0.27
eV, as we shall see,
N
el
can be adjusted from 0 to 1 and then to 2. We take the
μ
leads
= - 0.4
eV as an example to demonstrate the control procedure, as shown in Fig. 3(b).

For
U < 0.22
eV,
μ ( 1 )
is greater than
μ
leads
and thus there is no electron filled into the degenerate shell. As U increases,
μ ( 1 )
and
μ ( 2 )
decrease while
μ
leads
keeps constant. At
U ≈
0.22 eV,
μ ( 1 )
is approximately equal to
μ
leads
and a resonant conductance occurs. The electron transport must satisfy the condition that
μ
S
⩾ μ ( 1 ) ⩾
μ
D
. For 0.22 eV
< U <
0.46 eV,
μ ( 1 ) <
μ
leads
< μ ( 2 )
and the electron transport keeps closed. Meanwhile, the system satisfies
N
el
= 1
and
S = 1 / 2
. At
U ≈
0.46 eV,
μ ( 2 )
is approximately equal to
μ
leads
and the resonant conductance occurs again. For 0.46 eV
< U <
0.5 eV, the electron transport is closed. Moreover, the system satisfies
N
el
= 2
and
S = 1
. These results show that the electron number
N
el
and the total spin S can be controlled by the electric field. The findings support the possibility to use the system as a spin memory device. Moreover, the single-electron transport through the dot can be switched by the electric field. On the other hand, our results imply that external electric fields can add ‘unexpected’ electrons to the dot and can also take out ‘useful’ electrons from the dot when the dot is used as the device with a fixed
N
el
.

For the TGQD with up to two electrons filled in the degenerate shell, we have calculated the eigenstates and the chemical potentials as functions of the electric field. When the electric field is weaker, there are many anticrossings between the levels of the shell, which implies that the corresponding eigenstates mix with each other. As the electric field becomes stronger, the levels tend to be linear, which implies that the corresponding eigenstates tend to be stable. In the single-electron system, the ground level decreases linearly with the electric field and the ground state remains
S = 1 / 2
. In the two-electron system, the ground level also decreases with the electric field and the ground state remains
S = 1
. Based on analyzing the chemical potentials, we present the control scheme of both the shell filling and the total spin by the electric field. The single-electron transport can also be switched by the electric field. The results support the possibility to use the system as a spin memory device or an electron-transport switch.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 11604183 and 11674197), and a Project of Shandong Province Higher Educational Science and Technology Program (Grant No. J16LJ09).
